label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's try to figure this out. So the question is whether following these steps will bring me back to the starting point. The instructions are: always face forward. Then take 10 steps backward, then 7 steps backward, then 8 steps right, 6 steps right, and finally 3 steps left. The options are Yes or No. First, I need to visualize this. Let me imagine I'm standing at the starting point, facing forward. When I take steps backward, that would be in the opposite direction of where I'm facing. So if I take 10 steps backward, I'm moving away from the starting point in the backward direction. Then taking 7 more steps backward adds to that movement. So in total, for the backward direction, that's 10 + 7 = 17 steps backward. Now, for the sideways movements: 8 steps to the right and then 6 steps to the right. Since right is the same direction, these add up. So 8 + 6 = 14 steps to the right. Then there's 3 steps to the left. Left is the opposite of right, so subtracting those steps from the rightward movement. So 14 steps right minus 3 steps left would be 11 steps to the right overall. So, in total, I've moved 17 steps backward and 11 steps to the right. Since backward and right are perpendicular directions (assuming standard orientation where forward is along one axis and right is along another), these movements form a right-angled triangle. The total displacement from the starting point would be the hypotenuse of this triangle. Calculating the distance, using the Pythagorean theorem: sqrt(17² + 11²) = sqrt(289 + 121) = sqrt(410) ≈ 20.248 steps. Since this is not zero, I haven't returned to the starting point. Therefore, the answer should be No. Wait, but maybe I made a mistake. Let me check again. First,
